episode_transcript:
[00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:28.670] Tesla's a very high beta stock, and if the market continues falling, Tesla will most likely continue falling along with it, and probably more severely. Welcome to Investing Experts, Pacifica Yield and The Value Edge. We are getting into Tesla stock today.
[00:00:28.830 --> 00:00:56.010] A perfect day, I think, to cover the stock, given the delivery numbers they just announced and how the market is taking it. Welcome to the show. First of all, it's great to talk to you on Seeking Alpha. Thank you. It's great to be here. Value Edge, talk to us about what you thought of Tesla's numbers today and broadly speaking, how you see Tesla. Yeah, so obviously the numbers today, they missed the market expectations a bit.
[00:00:56.090 --> 00:01:19.110] I think we saw it dip down quite significantly before market opened, and then it was sort of a mixed reaction as the market went. You can't really argue there's been some brand damage over the past few months. I think Elon Musk, his involvement in politics has definitely, it's obviously been a controversial topic.
[00:01:19.110 --> 00:01:44.470] And I think he disenfranchised, and I wrote on this recently, I think he disenfranchised a portion of his existing customer base. And so you probably didn't see that in this report. You may see it in future production delivery reports. But I think the important part is they're producing, what is it, 362,000 vehicles in Q1. You look back, these are near record numbers, if not record numbers.
[00:01:44.470 --> 00:02:12.830] I mean, five years ago, this was a very small company in terms of total production. So the production rate is growing. Obviously in 23, we had some pretty material margin compression. But I think the unit economics are recovering again. We're seeing that in more recent reports. So it's not great. And it definitely highlights the environment we're in with sort of weakening EV demand, a weakening auto market, maybe a weakening consumer.
[00:02:14.330 --> 00:02:41.850] But from the fundamental perspective of the company, right, you've got over the course of five years, they've probably more than doubled production. I don't know the exact metrics. I'm going to pull it up on the side here. But they're growing their production quite significantly. They've got a global manufacturing footprint. They've changed sort of how the auto manufacturing process works from their gigacasting to, right, all the automation or the actual human touch they involve in there.
[00:02:41.850 --> 00:03:09.250] So I think it's still a fundamentally good story. You've got growth. But it's just, you know, there's some weak points. And there's quite a bit of brand damage. I think that the company is going to be working through for quite a bit of time here. You can't really avoid that with Elon's activities and his extracurriculars. I don't think it's the end of the world. It obviously wasn't what the market was looking for. But, you know, I think you look five years out, that production number is going to continue growing.
[00:03:09.250 --> 00:03:37.210] That production number, the unit economics are probably going to continue improving at least or stay, you know, flat. But this company has a very good track record of improving their unit economics. So I think it's sort of fine. You know, I think you just look out a few years and this will be the result of a weak macro environment in a somewhat weak consumer with, you know, a little bit of extracurriculars added in there. And I think, you know, four or five years from now, it's probably a distant memory.
[00:03:37.590 --> 00:04:05.470] Let me ask you, before I ask Pacifica Yield to share his bearish take on the stock, we saw those disappointing numbers, but then we saw the stock rise on the day. Are you of the opinion that that has to do with the report that came out that ostensibly Elon Musk will be taking away from his Doge role and putting more time into his Tesla role? Are you of the opinion that that's the thing that pushed it up today?
[00:04:05.470 --> 00:04:33.970] Or what are your thoughts on why it's up despite disappointing numbers? Yeah, I would say you're probably right. I would think less government involvement is a net positive, a net benefit for the stock, right? Because investors care about the business. And if Elon's extracurriculars are hurting the brand reputation, brand value, that's going to have very real impacts on the business, right? We see people are going out in the real world and damaging Teslas, they're damaging dealerships.
[00:04:34.310 --> 00:04:59.390] None of this is good for a business. So I think that takes a big bit of uncertainty out of it. And it also will help it sort of fade out of memory over time of Elon's intense involvement in a very contentious administration. You're sort of demonizing yourself to half of your population and making a hero out of yourself out of the other half.
[00:04:59.550 --> 00:05:29.190] But I think from a business perspective, yeah, there's going to be more focus and a lot less news headlines, I'm hoping, from an investor's standpoint. Yeah. Pacifica Yield, what are your thoughts in general and given the numbers that came out today? What are your bearish thoughts predicated on? Thanks, Irina. So I think our startup, I say, and I think Tesla is a great company. It's a great American car company. And essentially, I've been following Elon since 2017.
[00:05:29.190 --> 00:05:57.350] My first article on Tesla was back in 2017 and I actually recommended it as a buy. I think since then what we've seen is a huge divergence of value from fundamentals. So the current market cap, I think, has sort of hugely diverged from its fundamental value. And the numbers were, as Danai said from Wedbush, it's sort of very much catastrophic. It was a disaster, underperformed consensus by a huge margin.
[00:05:57.490 --> 00:06:23.970] And it's down from the previous two fiscal years. And those kind of numbers in a single period is very hard to recover from. And I think it's very much a brand image problem. And with Elon at Doge, I think the Politico article that came out around him even quite soon was, he mentioned that was fake news. He tweeted out that a few hours ago. So he's very much still involved politically.
[00:06:24.070 --> 00:06:50.970] And I think that it will be an abatross around Tesla's neck for as long as we sort of go through this period of political uncertainty. And my view is a year from now, in terms of sales and demand, even though production has gone up, sales and demand probably will dip. When you see the reciprocal tariffs being brought in, for example, I think it's on the EU, it's 20%. And on the UK, which is Tesla's largest market in Europe, it's 10%.
[00:06:50.970 --> 00:07:20.130] There will be a response, not just from the consumers, but from the governments of these countries. So on a global perspective, we will see a large retrenchment of sales in the EU, definitely in the UK, in Australia and possibly in Asia as well, China and less so. And in the US, it's the core market. I think we will see a weaker consumer demand just from potential recession. I think the Atlanta Fed has forecast a negative GDP growth of roughly 3.8%.
[00:07:20.130 --> 00:07:49.190] And it's just not the macro environment that people will go out to buy cars in the tens of thousands. So I think from the macro side, Tesla does face some headwinds. And the valuation against these headwinds to me just doesn't make sense. It has a combined market cap in excess of the largest 10 automakers combined. And that will not hold in a recession if the US starts following a recession, which I think David Morgan and Goldman have ramped up the expectations in 2025.
[00:07:49.970 --> 00:08:19.770] So definitely, I think we'll see some headwinds for Tesla. It's still a great company. But the valuation is just not at the right stage right now. And there's a lot of room for that to come down. You mentioned Elon Musk denied that report. Do you think that we're going to see tomorrow and in the ensuing days, it get back to, you know, a lower valuation? What's your thinking on how the Elon Musk part of it affects things in the near term? I think the near term is going to be down.
[00:08:20.430 --> 00:08:46.810] The S&P will over the next few, from my perspective, I've sort of started to position my portfolio for this. But I think the S&P will drop down on that broad market sell off on the back of the tariffs and what might be a trade war that he has recommended. It's bazooka options to respond to Trump and that overall sentiment of stagflation is going to ramp up in the market.
[00:08:47.110 --> 00:09:15.630] So I think in the near term, we'll see Tesla drop tomorrow when the market opens and over the next few weeks. And the only thing I can see is to reverse that as if we see the last minute deals that we saw delay the Canadian and the Mexico tariffs. But I think definitely sentiment from the market is going to be set. And we will see Tesla as a high profile casualty of that just because of his closeness to the 47th president and this current administration.
[00:09:16.270 --> 00:09:42.670] And what would you say to the value edges point and many bullish perspectives that what they have coming down the pike is going to set them up for the long term and long term? It's still a bullish scenario. And to add to that point, there's much conversation around Tesla's consumers. Maybe for the most part, those consumers are are not necessarily affected by a recession, even if we do get one.
[00:09:42.670 --> 00:10:09.950] I think the core thing for Tesla is even though it's production is, I think a core dip we saw in the first quarter, a lot of it was because the model Y was down for a refresh. So we might see some some demand return from there. But really, it's a image problem. And a car isn't just like so the there was in 2024, those boycott of I think it was Bud Light, but Bud Light is for ten dollars.
[00:10:10.290 --> 00:10:39.490] A car is probably the second most expensive item someone will buy outside of their home. So this demand simply will not return that easily. So even though we do have a huge amount of downstream production capacity expansion, the demand itself there, I think Belgium, it's down 70 percent. In Denmark, it's down more than 40 percent. In France, it's down more than 40 percent. In the UK, which was probably the only positive market, it had a trade war might affect those figures.
[00:10:39.750 --> 00:11:09.130] So I think in terms of its downstream pipeline, even though production will ramp up, Tesla will see a lot of its demand, especially in its core European markets. And even in the US, large democratic states like California, New York, Massachusetts, we might see a dip there just because of political ramifications of Elon. So I do think we'll see Tesla have to offer more exceptions, more discounts, more deals to boost that demand.
[00:11:09.310 --> 00:11:39.030] We've already seen it. So Tesla offer us zero percent financing. We will see more price cuts and that will have a negative impact on margins. But overall, the company will have to be very, very aggressive on on sort of discounting and bills and ad hoc policies it can implement to boost demand. But I don't see I just do not see demand returning to its levels I had in 2023 and 2024. And the value edge, how would you answer those points?
[00:11:39.250 --> 00:12:03.950] Can't disagree with anything. Right. He's he's bringing facts and numbers. I guess what I'd say in terms of consumer behavior in a recession. Right. Cars are a very unique product. If my car breaks down tomorrow, whether we are in the worst recession since 08 or in a booming economy, I need a new car. Right. I think a lot of that demand, it doesn't disappear. It will shift towards used autos.
[00:12:03.950 --> 00:12:27.430] And actually, funny enough, from the bullish perspective, used autos, the used Tesla market right now, I think, is plummeting. It is not doing well. And I think a lot of that, again, ties back into the brand damage type conversation we've been having in terms of the mind's eye of the consumer. Right. When you have these auto import tariffs that we've been hearing and hearing about and seeing all these headlines for. Right.
[00:12:27.570 --> 00:12:53.570] Tesla's a bit more shielded from that because they've got a much more domestic manufacturing base for their U.S. EVs than any other EV manufacturer producing cars locally here. Right. Not to mention Tesla already has profitable EV manufacturing footprint in the U.S. I don't think there's any other current automaker making electric vehicles profitably right now.
[00:12:53.770 --> 00:13:15.150] So you're going to see car prices increase, but Tesla's rate of increase will be less that than their competitors. I think that will influence some some consumer purchasing decisions. Right. Right. But to Pacifica's point. Right. There's going to be deals. There's going to probably be margin compression to bolster some demand. Right.
[00:13:15.610 --> 00:13:43.550] Tesla has a little bit of room to to eat some of that margin margin compression, but it's not going to be welcome news from the market and from investors. Right. We saw that in 2023. The market is very averse to margin compression. But this is where I think the conversation sort of shifts more to Tesla's ecosystem as a whole. You've got the supercharger network. So you've got a little bit of, you know, the inkling of a very durable revenue stream in the future.
[00:13:43.550 --> 00:14:11.890] You've got autonomy, which is still several years, in my opinion, away from being a material contributor. I think Elon is very, very ambitious on his goals. Right. I'd like to probably say three to five years we start seeing some sort of meaningful contribution. And then you've got all of these different projects happening internally between a U.S. lithium refinery, U.S. manufacturing footprint, the dojo chip architecture.
[00:14:11.890 --> 00:14:34.790] You've got these humongous training clusters for AI. I think that may actually be owned by XAI, but I assume internally things are pretty fluid between Elon's companies. Not to mention, right, you've got this whole wave of what do we think Optimus will be? That's going to really impact the multiple of the stock over time. Right.
[00:14:34.870 --> 00:15:01.190] Because if Optimus pans out in the way that Tesla, the company, has been saying it will, then it's obviously not really priced into the stock. I think because Elon is saying this is something of a multi-trillion dollar market, right? Humanoid robots and real world AI. How do you price that into a stock? It's impossible, right? So you just kind of guess. That's what the market's doing is what do we think the likelihood is and what do we think this could, you know, turn out to be?
[00:15:01.350 --> 00:15:28.230] I like to focus more on, you know, FSD progress and what the licensing of FSD might look like in the future. Right. That's sort of an NVIDIA competitor with NVIDIA's Drive platform. You've got a whole internal chip team developing and designing chips, getting them manufactured by leading edge chip manufacturers and foundries. And then you've just sort of have this full sail shift towards AI that the whole market has been obsessed with.
[00:15:28.490 --> 00:15:54.870] And you can think of very few companies better positioned to fully benefit from that than Tesla. Right. They've got a fleet of vehicles that are software defined vehicles. They're working on a humanoid robot. Whether it's five or ten years in the future, who knows? They're working on a semi truck, which, you know, ties into FSD quite well. FSD licensing you can have. Superchargers can get smarter. You've got home energy solutions.
[00:15:54.870 --> 00:16:24.710] So you sort of have this whole ecosystem of businesses that are not currently meaningful contributors. But five, ten years from now, the financial statements and the lines on the income statement might look a lot different in terms of your revenue share between different business lines within the company. So I think we're rightfully so for right now, because the auto business is a huge segment of Tesla overall. But I think we're very hyper focused on the auto segment.
[00:16:24.910 --> 00:16:52.530] And that's what analysts typically are very, very hyper focused on this. Right. Because right now it's a major contributor to their revenue. But then you see, you know, Tesla energy just exceeded 10 percent for the first time in Q1, I believe. And then all of this sort of the potential of the AI use cases that you've got in terms of real world AI. I think it's coming, you know, but it's just a matter of when. So FSD, for those that don't know, stands for full self drive.
[00:16:52.530 --> 00:17:20.390] I'm curious what your thoughts are near term. Are you also of the opinion that Tesla is going to go down in the very near term? And what are the, I guess, exact I mean, you mentioned a few of them just now, but what are the data points that you think will help turn it around on the long term? Or what should investors be paying attention to along the way? Like what would be a good data point and when would those be coming? Yeah, that's a great question.
[00:17:20.390 --> 00:17:48.550] So, I mean, frankly, I don't really know what's going to happen tomorrow or next month. I think it was a much easier call up at $450 with over, you know, a PE exceeding 100 X, right? Yeah, this stock is not going to remain that high for that long. I think I wrote a sell article, a sell rated article at 436, right? Felt pretty stupid as it was going up to 470. But now we're sitting back towards 250, 260. It's yeah, you know, in hindsight, it was pretty obvious.
[00:17:48.550 --> 00:18:15.330] Whether we have further to fall, maybe. I think Tesla is a very high beta stock. And if the market continues falling, Tesla will most likely continue falling along with it and probably more severely. To your second part of the question, which is what data points are going to drive a turnaround? Some sort of positive news out of auto demand and EV demand would help. I don't think that's coming in the next year. I think the auto market is in a pretty dismal point right now.
[00:18:15.330 --> 00:18:38.670] But this stock has always traded on the story and the potential and what do we think is going to happen? I think it's much more based on subjective just sentiment. What is the story and how is the market feeling, right? When we're trading at $450 and I write a sell rated article, everybody's telling me, no, this stock is just going to continue going up. There's no way, right? It's so sentiment driven.
[00:18:38.670 --> 00:19:07.650] So I think as soon as that sort of brand damage starts to fade back into distant memory as Elon gets further away from politics and more focused on driving product delivery and engineering improvements, I think it will show through over time. I just don't know how long that takes, right? It might take a year. It might take two years. If you're not patient enough, this is definitely not the stock for you. It's going to be very volatile.
[00:19:07.890 --> 00:19:35.770] But if you're willing to play it out, I think 10 years from now, we're looking at still one of the largest companies in the world and one of the most influential companies in the world. Being fairly valued. Does that apply here? How do you navigate the price targets? That's a great question as well. It's very hard to value a company like this. It's almost a fool's game because everything is driven by sentiment, really. I think over time, you look five, ten years back in the past, right?
[00:19:35.930 --> 00:19:57.950] You see phenomenal business growth and the stock follows that. But right now, to Pacifica's point, we're at a very elevated multiple, especially relative to other automakers if that's how you're pricing Tesla. We're not trading at fair value if you're pricing Tesla as a car company. We're probably trading beneath fair value or below fair value if you're pricing Tesla as a pure real-world AI play.
[00:19:58.950 --> 00:20:28.230] So it's just the uncertainty that's inherent in the stock and the beta that the stock carries makes it almost impossible to do any sort of reliable valuation analysis of it. So you just look at the multiples and you say, well, based on our multiples, this is clearly an overvalued stock. But based on the potential of the business, well, it might not be so overvalued, right? I think you have to take a very nuanced perspective with it. Not much room for nuance, though, these days, is there? No, definitely not. But we're all here to celebrate it.
[00:20:28.730 --> 00:20:55.850] Pacifica Yield, I'd ask you the same question. What are your thoughts on the valuation and how do you navigate that part of analyzing this stock? Yeah, so math has made a few great points around, especially around the non-automotive segment. So, like I said, so robotics, optimist, energy storage, AI do have a lot of long-term potential. And that's where the difficulty of Tesla comes from. It's not just valued as an automotive stock.
[00:20:55.990 --> 00:21:23.950] It's valued as a sort of play on, I guess, Elon's genius because you used to look at his wider portfolio of companies, SpaceX, Starlink, Neuralink, Boring Company. And you do see a man that is probably, in a sense, generational and investors do want to pay for that exposure. So it's hard. And so I would recommend people not to short Tesla because the valuation can detach very quickly based on whatever news comes.
[00:21:24.210 --> 00:21:52.810] But from a fundamental perspective, the company probably is overvalued to the extent of 100%. If I had to give a specific figure just on the automotive segment, it's still been by far the largest driver of revenues. I think it's in excess of 80%. Tesla's great story, especially around the cash flows it's received from auto segment, has been a huge driver of its value gain since the pandemic.
[00:21:53.410 --> 00:22:14.610] So once we see that start to reverse and we possibly see free cash flow start to come in negative, which would be a huge jump from where it currently is now, then the market would be less willing to pay for that sort of great premium or the Elon premium. And we will see some of that valuation start to pull back and Tesla be more valued as is to the car company than it currently is now.
[00:22:15.610 --> 00:22:38.770] But yeah, long term, definitely FSD, which is still three or five years away, possibly longer in European countries with a lot more stringent regulations. Optimus would be an incredible product if it does actually come in the market. But energy storage is very interesting because it's been a huge driver of revenues historically. I think, like Matt said, it's roughly 10% of revenue for the last quarter.
[00:22:39.170 --> 00:23:04.870] But with uncertainty around the Inflation Reduction Act, it's broadly speaking, I'm very involved in investing in utilities and green energy. There's been quite a few retrenchments in the area. So Trump is looking at boosting production of coal. So if that does happen and there's a reduction in demand for solar and wind, we'll see energy storage in the US for that segment specifically start to go down.
[00:23:04.870 --> 00:23:24.090] So for Tesla, its core, most near-term revenue generating segments and autos and energy storage do have more near-term headwind than they do tillwind. But over the next decade plus, AI, robotics, FSD, then devaluation, I think it's a lot harder to pin down.
[00:23:24.090 --> 00:23:47.230] The market will be more harsh in the near-term just because we're entering a huge risk-off period, which will reflect what it was when the Fed started raising rates in 2022. Because in 2021, where there was a SPAC bubble, any company in any industry could go public and it would go up to 300% within a week. Because that's the risk-off sentiment just didn't exist.
[00:23:47.590 --> 00:24:17.150] And if we do go back to that, where there's talks of the Fed possibly raising rates, inflation starts to go up again, we will see Tesla's valuation more heavily retrenched. So that is my base case for the company really. There's this in the next two years, medium term and in shorter term, next six months, we will see more downward volatility. But that valuation is a lot harder to pin down over the next five plus years.
[00:24:18.090 --> 00:24:47.330] Is there something that would turn you bullish or some things that would turn you bullish at this point? I think it would be hard for me to join the bullish camp just because the brand damage is very difficult to recover from. And it might be that in the next two years, the politics becomes a lot less prevalent, especially as Doge is set to terminate before the next midterm elections.
[00:24:48.430 --> 00:25:14.050] But just because if we do see an entrenched level of trade wars, countries possibly fall into a recession. I think there's projections for Canada to fall into a recession. It would be hard for people to go back to the overall demand that Tesla saw in 2024 because the brand would be permanently associated with that sort of destruction of value and destruction of jobs.
[00:25:14.530 --> 00:25:42.710] Because in Canada specifically, there's been thousands of jobs already lost in this of the still industry. And if that trade war does ramp up, then again, it's just hard to see how the brand recovers in the country. It is definitely that. Tesla isn't just an American. It doesn't just sell to the US and the US is its largest market. It sells to China. It sells to the UK. And I think from that global perspective, especially as Trump has ramped up the trade war against China, there will be global brand damage.
[00:25:42.810 --> 00:26:11.710] And those tariffs are not just going to go away. It's very much a central part of the Trump administration for the next four years or maybe two years until they lose in the midterms if they do. So it's just that I think we have a permanent trade war for the next four years. And Tesla within that scope will be definitely in the near term always associated with that. I just don't see that brand really recovering. It's not just... And the same thing happened to a few brands in the past.
[00:26:11.770 --> 00:26:39.810] You look at the Bud Light and the issue it had last year. The brands still haven't recovered. And a car being a huge investment for most people, I just do not see in the near term how Tesla really overcomes the current headwinds and overcomes the current damage to its brand, especially within its core demographics. Thoughts on Tesla's competitors and who may pick up the slack, I guess, especially pertaining to the car angle? Yes.
[00:26:40.030 --> 00:27:08.870] So it will definitely depend on the specific markets. We will see a rise in... I think what we've seen in Canada where it was the buy Canadian ramp up. But Canada doesn't really have its own domestic car manufacturing company. But from a global perspective, we will see... My base bearish case is that we will see essentially the same thing happen in other countries by Germany or by China or by the UK. So we will see demand increase of BYD products in China, for example, and possibly more near-term softness.
[00:27:09.070 --> 00:27:36.530] For Tesla in the EU, more German and French car companies will see a boost. I think they've seen in the first quarter there was a rise in sales, essentially, for those car companies compared to Tesla. And from a global perspective, yes, there will be more competition within the US. Tesla has done an incredible job in basing its domestic manufacturing base in the US and hiring Americans.
[00:27:36.710 --> 00:28:05.150] That's something I will actually commend Tesla for. That's a great, great, great position to be in with Trump's tariffs. But from an international perspective, I think we will see a lot of weakness and how that translates to within a trade war. How that weakness evolves or retrenches, I think will be interesting to see. But from an international perspective, I think we've seen Tesla, the best days of it is very much over.
[00:28:05.710 --> 00:28:32.130] And I don't think we'll see sales ever recover to how they were in 2024 or 2023 in basically every single international market. That means something for the company. A lot of great points made hitting the nail on the head. From a domestic standpoint, in the US market, I think it's indisputable. Tesla is the clear leader in EVs. The Model Y is one of the best-selling, I think, if not the best-selling car in the world, right?
[00:28:32.230 --> 00:28:59.710] You've got sort of these fundamental tailwinds from just the adoption of EVs. It's sort of you've got all your other automakers are at a steady state growth rate for decades. And then you've got a new market with EVs, and it's taking share from traditional internal combustion vehicles. And then you've got the clear market leader. That's a great position to be in. But then to Pacifica's point, Tesla is not just a domestic company. They're not just a US company. It's an international brand.
[00:29:00.230 --> 00:29:29.370] They've got huge production in China, which if that Shanghai gigafactory is used as a sort of negotiating factor, that would turn me bearish almost immediately, right? If we saw some rhetoric out of Xi Jinping and the CCP that American business is not as welcome anymore as a result of the sort of very protectionist trade policy that the US is turning towards, right? That could have a significant impact on this stock.
[00:29:29.510 --> 00:29:57.590] There's no avoiding that. That's just a reality, right? And again, in Europe, I believe, again, to Pacifica's point, Tesla sales are not doing all that well. You've got Gigafactory Berlin has been experiencing some outages last year. This year, it's experienced some between protests and other just planned outages. From an international standpoint and looking at the auto market as a whole, right, it doesn't look great for the next year.
[00:29:57.850 --> 00:30:22.830] And I think looking at any sort of political backlash from any international country where Tesla has major production facilities, so that for me would be China and Germany, that would be very bad. You know, there's really no avoiding that. And if you're a bull, there's no point in trying to shield yourself from that reality. I think it just returns back to this conversation of what does the product pipeline look like?
[00:30:22.990 --> 00:30:49.350] And if you've got the best EV in the US, which I think Tesla has, right, a lot of Chinese competitors are cost competitive and performance competitive with Tesla at this point. But in the US, that's a different story. And then you add on to it these ancillary products like energy storage and home charging and a supercharger network. I think over time, you sort of see people want that Tesla ecosystem, not just the Tesla product, right?
[00:30:49.510 --> 00:31:18.770] So another thing that is going to be a huge focus for me this year is trying to quantify in whatever way possible what this brand damage is doing to the company's demand profile. Because that's, bar none, I think the most important thing to monitor over 2025 is really what is the extent of how much I think, you know, there's no avoiding it. Mostly people on the democratic side, mostly people with democratic political beliefs, how much do they hate Tesla now?
[00:31:18.770 --> 00:31:43.830] I think finding the answer to that question, there's going to be some discovery in the market this year. A bull (The Value Edge) and bear (Pacifica Yield) discuss Tesla. Brand damage and Q1 deliveries (0:55). Are TSLA's plans for the future enough to be long-term bullish on the stock? (9:20) What data points will drive a turnaround? (17:10) How to fairly value Tesla (19:20). Tesla's competitors (26:35). Tesla ecosystem as a whole (33:35)
